statement: Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (Viðreisn) first introduced a parliamentary resolution for an EU accession referendum on 19 September 2023 (153. löggjafarþing), when Viðreisn was in opposition. The 2023 proposal used similar framing to the 2026 version — asking whether negotiations should continue — and argued that the Icelandic public had been denied the right to complete the process that began with the 2009 application. The resolution did not pass as Viðreisn lacked a parliamentary majority. Logi Einarsson (Samfylkingin) had introduced an earlier version on 20 September 2022 (153. löggjafarþing) with the same essential question. These repeated proposals demonstrate that the referendum concept was developed over multiple parliamentary sessions before the 2024 coalition agreement formalised it as government policy.
source_name: Alþingi — Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (flutningsræða, 154. löggjafarþing); Logi Einarsson (flutningsræða, 153. löggjafarþing)
caveats: The consistency of the referendum proposals across sessions demonstrates a deliberate political strategy by pro-EU parties. However, the failure of earlier proposals also shows that the referendum only became possible after the 2024 election produced a coalition willing to pursue it. The wording of the question evolved slightly between iterations.

related_entries.statement: The coalition government formed in late 2024, comprising Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), agreed in their coalition agreement to hold a referendum on whether to resume EU accession negotiations by 2027. The referendum was subsequently scheduled for 29 August 2026. The question asks whether Iceland should resume accession negotiations with the EU — not whether to join. This is the first Icelandic government since Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (2009–2013) to actively pursue EU-related policy. Prime Minister Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin) and Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (Viðreisn) have been the primary government figures advancing the referendum. Previous governments, including those led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson (2013–2016) and Katrín Jakobsdóttir (2017–2024), did not pursue EU accession despite intermittent public calls for a referendum.

Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir presented a parliamentary resolution (stjórnartillaga til þingsályktunar) on 9 March 2026 proposing a referendum on 29 August 2026 with the question: 'Á að halda áfram viðræðum um aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu?' (Should negotiations on Iceland's EU accession continue?). In her flutningsræða (sponsor's speech), she framed the referendum as a sovereignty issue — arguing that allowing the nation to decide is itself an exercise of sovereignty. She drew parallels with three historical decisions (NATO membership, EFTA accession, EEA agreement) that were also contentious at the time but are now widely regarded as beneficial. She stated that Iceland's 2009 EU application remains valid, confirmed by the EU itself, and that negotiations could resume 'around the turn of the year' if the public votes yes. She explicitly pledged: 'Ég mun aldrei skrifa undir samning sem tryggir ekki okkar markmið og okkar yfirráð' (I will never sign a deal that does not secure our goals and our sovereignty) — specifically regarding fisheries. She cited precedents for special arrangements: Nordic agricultural exemptions (Sweden, Finland) and property purchase restrictions (Denmark, Malta).

The current Icelandic government is a coalition of Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), holding 32 of 63 Althingi seats. The Prime Minister is Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin). This is the first government coalition where both explicitly pro-EU parties (Samfylkingin and Viðreisn) serve together, which has been cited as a factor in advancing the EU referendum.

citations.report_title: Enginn afsláttur gefinn segi þjóðin já við aðildarviðræðum

claims.claim_text: Þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan um ESB-aðild var kynnt í þingsályktunartillögu sem ríkisstjórnin lagði fram á föstudaginn 6. mars 2026.
Í þingsályktunartillögunni er þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan ákveðin þannig að spurt verður: "Á að halda áfram viðræðum um aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu?"
Ísland mun geta greiðt atkvæði aftur um ESB-aðild að loknum aðildarviðræðum, segi þjóðin já í fyrstu þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni.
Þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan er ákveðin þannig að hún er ekki atkvæðagreiðsla um ESB-aðild í heild, heldur um hvort hafist verði handa við aðildarviðræður.
